---
title: "DATA 608 Story 6: What Is The State of Food Security and Nutrition in the US?"
subtitle: "A Data Brief for Policy Makers and Advocates"
author: "Joao De Oliveira"
date: 5/11/2026
format:
  pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: false
    colorlinks: true
    fig-pos: "H"
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{float}
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
jupyter: python3
---

## Story Development Focus

**Focus points addressed:** Audience · Story Framework · Slide Messaging Technique · Visual Selection Framework

This story is written for a **political audience — legislators and policy advocates** being lobbied to address food insecurity in the United States. Every design and messaging decision flows from that context:

| Decision | Rationale for a Political Audience |
|----------|-----------------------------------|
| Bold headline findings | Politicians respond to clear, quotable numbers — not nuance-first framing |
| State-level choropleth | Politicians represent geographies; a map makes the issue local and personal |
| Poverty-vs-insecurity scatter | Shows the causal chain that justifies policy intervention |
| Child-to-adult pipeline chart | Answers the "what happens if we do nothing?" question directly |
| Consistent red/amber/green palette | Red = crisis, amber = at risk, green = secure — universally legible |
| `matplotlib` throughout | Single library; consistent, reproducible, professional look |

**Story framework:** Scale of the problem → Where it is worst → Who suffers most → The poverty connection → The cost of inaction (children) → The ask.

**Audience note:** This brief uses the same data the USDA presents to Congress. Every number is sourced and citable.

---

## Data Sources

| Dataset | Source | Period |
|---------|--------|--------|
| State-level food insecurity rates | USDA ERS, 3-year avg 2022–2024 | 2022–2024 |
| National food insecurity trend | USDA ERS Household Food Security Survey | 2001–2024 |
| Food insecurity by demographic group | USDA ERS ERR-358 / ERR-337 | 2024 |
| Poverty–food insecurity relationship | USDA ERS ERR-358, Table 2 | 2024 |
| Child food insecurity rates | USDA ERS / Feeding America Map the Meal Gap 2024 | 2022–2024 |
| Long-term effects of child food insecurity | Fertig (2020), Health Affairs (2016), ScienceDirect (2024) | peer-reviewed |
| SNAP program spending | USDA FNS Annual Report FY2024 | FY2024 |

---

```{python}
#| label: install
import subprocess, sys

def ensure(pkg):
    try:
        __import__(pkg)
    except ImportError:
        subprocess.check_call([sys.executable, "-m", "pip", "install", pkg, "-q"])

for pkg in ["pandas", "matplotlib", "numpy", "scipy"]:
    ensure(pkg)
```

```{python}
#| label: setup
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches
import matplotlib.ticker as mticker
import matplotlib.colors as mcolors
from scipy import stats
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

# Palette
RED    = "#C0392B"   # crisis / food insecure
AMBER  = "#E67E22"   # at risk / moderate insecurity
GREEN  = "#1A7A4A"   # food secure
BLUE   = "#2471A3"   # children
PURPLE = "#7D3C98"   # policy / intervention
BG     = "#FAFAF8"
MUTED  = "#6B6B66"
BORDER = "#DCDBD3"

plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.facecolor" : BG,
    "axes.facecolor"   : BG,
    "axes.edgecolor"   : BORDER,
    "axes.grid"        : True,
    "grid.color"       : BORDER,
    "grid.linewidth"   : 0.5,
    "grid.alpha"       : 0.7,
    "font.family"      : "DejaVu Sans",
    "text.color"       : "#1A1A18",
    "axes.labelcolor"  : MUTED,
    "xtick.color"      : MUTED,
    "ytick.color"      : MUTED,
})

SOURCE_USDA   = "Source: USDA Economic Research Service, Household Food Security in the United States 2024 (ERR-358)"
SOURCE_STATE  = "Source: USDA ERS, 3-year average 2022-2024. State-level estimates combined for statistical reliability."
SOURCE_FA     = "Source: Feeding America, Map the Meal Gap 2024; USDA ERS ERR-358"
SOURCE_PEER   = "Sources: Fertig (2020), Panel Study of Income Dynamics; Health Affairs (2016); ScienceDirect meta-review (2024)"
```

\newpage

## 47 Million Americans Are Going Hungry — Right Here, Right Now

Food insecurity is not an abstract global problem. It is happening in every congressional district, in every state, in every community in America. As of 2024, **13.7 percent of US households — 18.3 million families — could not reliably put food on the table**.

```{python}
#| label: fig-trend
#| fig-cap: "National prevalence of food insecurity and very low food security in US households, 2001–2024. Source: USDA ERS Annual Household Food Security Survey."
#| fig-height: 5.0
#| fig-width: 10

years = list(range(2001, 2025))
food_insecure = [
    10.7, 11.1, 11.2, 11.9, 11.0, 10.9, 11.1, 14.6, 14.7, 14.5,
    14.9, 14.5, 14.3, 14.0, 12.7, 11.8, 11.1, 10.5, 10.5, 10.5,
    10.2, 12.8, 13.5, 13.7
]
very_low = [
    3.3, 3.5, 3.5, 3.9, 3.9, 4.1, 4.1, 5.7, 5.6, 5.4,
    5.7, 5.6, 5.6, 5.4, 4.8, 4.3, 4.5, 3.8, 3.9, 3.9,
    3.8, 5.1, 5.1, 5.4
]

df_trend = pd.DataFrame({"year": years, "insecure": food_insecure, "very_low": very_low})

fig1, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5.0))
fig1.patch.set_facecolor(BG)

ax1.fill_between(df_trend["year"], df_trend["insecure"],
                 alpha=0.18, color=AMBER, zorder=2)
ax1.fill_between(df_trend["year"], df_trend["very_low"],
                 alpha=0.25, color=RED, zorder=3)
ax1.plot(df_trend["year"], df_trend["insecure"],
         color=AMBER, linewidth=2.2, zorder=4, label="Food insecure (any severity)")
ax1.plot(df_trend["year"], df_trend["very_low"],
         color=RED, linewidth=2.2, zorder=4, label="Very low food security (reduced intake)")

# key events
for yr, label, y_val, color in [
    (2008, "2008\nFinancial\nCrisis", 14.6, RED),
    (2020, "COVID\n2020", 10.5, MUTED),
    (2022, "Post-COVID\nsurge", 12.8, AMBER),
    (2024, "2024:\n13.7%", 13.7, AMBER),
]:
    ax1.annotate(label, xy=(yr, y_val),
                 xytext=(yr + 0.3, y_val + 1.5),
                 fontsize=7.5, color=color, fontweight="bold",
                 arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", color=color, lw=1.0))

ax1.set_xlabel("Year", fontsize=10, color=MUTED)
ax1.set_ylabel("% of US households", fontsize=10, color=MUTED)
ax1.set_xlim(2001, 2025)
ax1.set_ylim(0, 20)
ax1.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda v, _: f"{v:.0f}%"))
ax1.legend(fontsize=9, framealpha=0.7, edgecolor=BORDER, loc="upper right")
ax1.spines[["top","right"]].set_visible(False)
ax1.spines[["left","bottom"]].set_color(BORDER)
ax1.set_axisbelow(True)

fig1.text(0.08, 0.97, "STORY 6  ·  DATA 608  ·  KNOWLEDGE & VISUAL ANALYTICS",
          fontsize=8.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig1.transFigure)
fig1.text(0.08, 0.925, "Food insecurity is rising again — and higher than pre-pandemic levels",
          fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", va="top", transform=fig1.transFigure)
fig1.text(0.08, 0.875, "Annual % of US households experiencing food insecurity  |  2001–2024",
          fontsize=9.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig1.transFigure)
fig1.text(0.08, 0.02, SOURCE_USDA,
          fontsize=7.5, color=MUTED, va="bottom", transform=fig1.transFigure)

plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.04, 1, 0.865])
plt.show()
```

**The trend line is moving in the wrong direction.** After years of progress, food insecurity jumped from 10.2% in 2021 to 13.7% in 2024 — a 34% increase in just three years, driven by food price inflation and the expiration of pandemic-era assistance programs.

\newpage

## The South Bears the Heaviest Burden — But No State Is Immune

```{python}
#| label: fig-state-map
#| fig-cap: "Food insecurity prevalence by state, 3-year average 2022-2024. States colored by severity. Source: USDA ERS."
#| fig-height: 5.2
#| fig-width: 10

# food insecurity rates by state, USDA ERS 3-year avg 2022-2024
# Source: https://www.ers.usda.gov/topics/food-nutrition-assistance/food-security-in-the-us
state_fi = {
    "AL":17.2,"AK":13.8,"AZ":14.1,"AR":19.4,"CA":12.3,
    "CO":11.2,"CT":10.8,"DE":11.9,"DC":15.3,"FL":14.8,
    "GA":15.7,"HI":11.0,"ID":12.8,"IL":12.5,"IN":13.4,
    "IA":10.5,"KS":12.0,"KY":16.2,"LA":17.8,"ME":11.6,
    "MD":10.3,"MA":10.1,"MI":13.9,"MN":10.0,"MS":18.9,
    "MO":14.3,"MT":12.5,"NE":10.8,"NV":13.5,"NH":9.5,
    "NJ":10.4,"NM":18.1,"NY":13.1,"NC":15.4,"ND":9.0,
    "OH":13.8,"OK":17.0,"OR":13.2,"PA":12.6,"RI":11.2,
    "SC":16.1,"SD":10.2,"TN":15.9,"TX":16.3,"UT":11.5,
    "VT":10.9,"VA":11.8,"WA":11.3,"WV":17.5,"WI":10.7,
    "WY":11.1,
}

STATE_COORDS = {
    "AL":(32.8,-86.8),"AK":(61.4,-152.0),"AZ":(34.3,-111.1),
    "AR":(34.9,-92.4),"CA":(36.8,-119.4),"CO":(39.0,-105.5),
    "CT":(41.6,-72.7),"DE":(39.0,-75.5),"DC":(38.9,-77.0),
    "FL":(27.8,-81.6),"GA":(32.7,-83.4),"HI":(20.9,-156.4),
    "ID":(44.4,-114.5),"IL":(40.0,-89.2),"IN":(40.3,-86.1),
    "IA":(42.0,-93.2),"KS":(38.5,-98.4),"KY":(37.5,-85.3),
    "LA":(31.2,-92.1),"ME":(45.4,-69.0),"MD":(39.0,-76.8),
    "MA":(42.3,-71.8),"MI":(44.3,-85.4),"MN":(46.4,-93.1),
    "MS":(32.7,-89.7),"MO":(38.5,-92.5),"MT":(46.9,-110.5),
    "NE":(41.5,-99.9),"NV":(39.3,-116.6),"NH":(43.7,-71.6),
    "NJ":(40.1,-74.5),"NM":(34.8,-106.2),"NY":(42.9,-75.6),
    "NC":(35.6,-79.4),"ND":(47.5,-100.5),"OH":(40.4,-82.8),
    "OK":(35.6,-96.9),"OR":(44.6,-122.1),"PA":(40.6,-77.2),
    "RI":(41.7,-71.5),"SC":(33.8,-80.9),"SD":(44.4,-100.2),
    "TN":(35.9,-86.7),"TX":(31.5,-99.3),"UT":(39.3,-111.5),
    "VT":(44.1,-72.7),"VA":(37.5,-78.5),"WA":(47.4,-120.6),
    "WV":(38.6,-80.5),"WI":(44.3,-89.8),"WY":(43.0,-107.6),
}

cmap = mcolors.LinearSegmentedColormap.from_list(
    "food_insecurity",
    ["#F9EBE9", "#E8A89C", AMBER, RED, "#7B241C"]
)
vmin, vmax = 9.0, 20.0
norm = mcolors.Normalize(vmin=vmin, vmax=vmax)

fig2, ax2 = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5.2))
fig2.patch.set_facecolor(BG)
ax2.set_facecolor("#EAF4FB")

try:
    import geopandas as gpd
    us_states = gpd.read_file(
        "https://naciscdn.org/naturalearth/110m/cultural/"
        "ne_110m_admin_1_states_provinces.zip"
    )
    us_states = us_states[us_states["admin"] == "United States of America"].copy()
    abbr_to_name = {
        "AL":"Alabama","AK":"Alaska","AZ":"Arizona","AR":"Arkansas","CA":"California",
        "CO":"Colorado","CT":"Connecticut","DE":"Delaware","DC":"District of Columbia",
        "FL":"Florida","GA":"Georgia","HI":"Hawaii","ID":"Idaho","IL":"Illinois",
        "IN":"Indiana","IA":"Iowa","KS":"Kansas","KY":"Kentucky","LA":"Louisiana",
        "ME":"Maine","MD":"Maryland","MA":"Massachusetts","MI":"Michigan","MN":"Minnesota",
        "MS":"Mississippi","MO":"Missouri","MT":"Montana","NE":"Nebraska","NV":"Nevada",
        "NH":"New Hampshire","NJ":"New Jersey","NM":"New Mexico","NY":"New York",
        "NC":"North Carolina","ND":"North Dakota","OH":"Ohio","OK":"Oklahoma","OR":"Oregon",
        "PA":"Pennsylvania","RI":"Rhode Island","SC":"South Carolina","SD":"South Dakota",
        "TN":"Tennessee","TX":"Texas","UT":"Utah","VT":"Vermont","VA":"Virginia",
        "WA":"Washington","WV":"West Virginia","WI":"Wisconsin","WY":"Wyoming",
    }
    fi_df = pd.DataFrame({
        "name": [abbr_to_name[k] for k in state_fi],
        "fi"  : list(state_fi.values())
    })
    name_col = "name" if "name" in us_states.columns else "NAME"
    us_states = us_states.merge(fi_df, left_on=name_col, right_on="name", how="left")
    us_states["color"] = us_states["fi"].apply(
        lambda v: mcolors.to_hex(cmap(norm(v))) if pd.notna(v) else "#CCCCCC")
    us_states.plot(ax=ax2, color=us_states["color"], edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.5)
    # highest states
    for abbr, label in [("AR","AR\n19.4%"),("MS","MS\n18.9%"),("NM","NM\n18.1%")]:
        lat, lon = STATE_COORDS[abbr]
        ax2.annotate(label, xy=(lon, lat), fontsize=6.5, fontweight="bold",
                     color="white", ha="center", va="center")
    use_shape = True
except Exception:
    use_shape = False
    for abbr, (lat, lon) in STATE_COORDS.items():
        fi = state_fi.get(abbr, None)
        if fi:
            ax2.scatter(lon, lat, c=[cmap(norm(fi))], s=110,
                        marker="s", zorder=3, linewidths=0)
            if abbr in ["AR","MS","NM","LA","WV"]:
                ax2.text(lon, lat-1.6, f"{abbr}\n{fi}%",
                         ha="center", fontsize=6, color="#7B241C", fontweight="bold")

ax2.set_xlim(-130, -65)
ax2.set_ylim(23, 52)
ax2.axis("off")

sm = plt.cm.ScalarMappable(cmap=cmap, norm=norm)
sm.set_array([])
cbar = fig2.colorbar(sm, ax=ax2, orientation="vertical",
                     fraction=0.025, pad=0.01, shrink=0.7)
cbar.set_label("% food insecure households", fontsize=9, color=MUTED)
cbar.ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda v, _: f"{v:.0f}%"))
cbar.ax.tick_params(labelsize=8, colors=MUTED)
cbar.outline.set_visible(False)

fig2.text(0.08, 0.97, "STORY 6  ·  DATA 608  ·  KNOWLEDGE & VISUAL ANALYTICS",
          fontsize=8.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig2.transFigure)
fig2.text(0.08, 0.925, "Arkansas (19.4%), Mississippi (18.9%), New Mexico (18.1%) lead the crisis",
          fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", va="top", transform=fig2.transFigure)
fig2.text(0.08, 0.875, "State food insecurity rate (3-year avg 2022-2024)  |  Darker red = more severe",
          fontsize=9.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig2.transFigure)
fig2.text(0.08, 0.02, SOURCE_STATE,
          fontsize=7.5, color=MUTED, va="bottom", transform=fig2.transFigure)

plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.04, 1, 0.865])
plt.show()
```

**Every state has a hunger problem.** The range runs from 9.0% in North Dakota to 19.4% in Arkansas — but even the "best" state means roughly 1 in 11 households struggling to eat. The South consistently carries the heaviest burden, with six states above 17%.

\newpage

## Who Is Most Vulnerable? Women, Children, and Single Parents

```{python}
#| label: fig-demographics
#| fig-cap: "Food insecurity prevalence by household type and demographic group, 2024. The dashed line marks the national average of 13.7%. Source: USDA ERS ERR-358, 2024."
#| fig-height: 5.5
#| fig-width: 10

groups = [
    "All households\n(national avg)",
    "Households\nwith children",
    "Single mother\nhouseholds",
    "Single father\nhouseholds",
    "Women\nliving alone",
    "Men\nliving alone",
    "Black\nhouseholds",
    "Hispanic\nhouseholds",
    "White non-\nHispanic HH",
    "Below\npoverty line",
    "Seniors\n65+ alone",
]
rates = [13.7, 18.4, 34.2, 23.3, 20.1, 15.3, 25.1, 20.8, 8.2, 39.4, 11.0]
colors = [AMBER if r < 20 else RED for r in rates]
colors[0] = MUTED
colors[-4] = RED   
colors[-2] = RED  

fig3, ax3 = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5.5))
fig3.patch.set_facecolor(BG)

x = range(len(groups))
bars = ax3.bar(x, rates, color=colors, width=0.65, zorder=3, alpha=0.88)

for bar, val in zip(bars, rates):
    ax3.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, val + 0.5,
             f"{val:.1f}%", ha="center", va="bottom",
             fontsize=9, fontweight="bold",
             color=RED if val >= 20 else AMBER if val >= 13.7 else MUTED)

# average reference line
ax3.axhline(13.7, color=MUTED, linewidth=1.5, linestyle="--", zorder=4)
ax3.text(len(groups)-0.4, 14.5, "National avg 13.7%",
         fontsize=8.5, color=MUTED, ha="right")

ax3.set_xticks(list(x))
ax3.set_xticklabels(groups, fontsize=8.5, rotation=20, ha="right")
ax3.set_ylabel("% food insecure", fontsize=10, color=MUTED)
ax3.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda v, _: f"{v:.0f}%"))
ax3.set_ylim(0, 48)
ax3.set_axisbelow(True)
ax3.spines[["top","right"]].set_visible(False)
ax3.spines[["left","bottom"]].set_color(BORDER)

leg = [
    mpatches.Patch(color=RED,   label="Severe (>= 20%)"),
    mpatches.Patch(color=AMBER, label="Above national avg"),
    mpatches.Patch(color=MUTED, label="National average"),
]
ax3.legend(handles=leg, loc="upper left", fontsize=9,
           framealpha=0.7, edgecolor=BORDER)

fig3.text(0.08, 0.97, "STORY 6  ·  DATA 608  ·  KNOWLEDGE & VISUAL ANALYTICS",
          fontsize=8.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig3.transFigure)
fig3.text(0.08, 0.925, "Nearly 2 in 5 households below the poverty line cannot reliably feed their families",
          fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", va="top", transform=fig3.transFigure)
fig3.text(0.08, 0.875,
          "Food insecurity by household type and demographic group, 2024",
          fontsize=9.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig3.transFigure)
fig3.text(0.08, 0.02, SOURCE_USDA,
          fontsize=7.5, color=MUTED, va="bottom", transform=fig3.transFigure)

plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.04, 1, 0.865])
plt.show()
```

**Single mothers face the steepest burden: 34.2% — more than 1 in 3.** Households below the poverty line reach 39.4%. These are not marginal cases — they represent millions of families for whom food is a daily source of stress and deprivation.

\newpage

## Poverty and Food Insecurity Are Inseparable

```{python}
#| label: fig-poverty-correlation
#| fig-cap: "Relationship between state poverty rate and food insecurity rate (2022-2024 averages). Each dot = one US state. Source: USDA ERS; US Census Bureau ACS 2022-2024."
#| fig-height: 5.5
#| fig-width: 10

# State poverty rates - US Census ACS 2022-2024 average
state_poverty = {
    "AL":17.5,"AK":10.3,"AZ":13.5,"AR":17.9,"CA":12.3,
    "CO":9.8, "CT":10.1,"DE":10.8,"DC":15.1,"FL":13.2,
    "GA":14.8,"HI":9.8, "ID":11.0,"IL":12.2,"IN":12.6,
    "IA":10.2,"KS":11.2,"KY":16.5,"LA":18.1,"ME":10.8,
    "MD":8.8, "MA":9.5, "MI":13.8,"MN":8.9, "MS":19.4,
    "MO":13.5,"MT":12.0,"NE":10.2,"NV":12.5,"NH":6.8,
    "NJ":9.1, "NM":18.5,"NY":13.5,"NC":14.3,"ND":9.5,
    "OH":13.9,"OK":16.2,"OR":12.0,"PA":12.5,"RI":10.5,
    "SC":15.8,"SD":10.8,"TN":14.5,"TX":14.8,"UT":8.9,
    "VT":9.5, "VA":9.8, "WA":10.5,"WV":17.8,"WI":9.5,
    "WY":10.2,
}

states = list(set(state_fi.keys()) & set(state_poverty.keys()))
pov  = [state_poverty[s] for s in states]
fi   = [state_fi[s] for s in states]

fig4, ax4 = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5.5))
fig4.patch.set_facecolor(BG)

sc = ax4.scatter(pov, fi, c=fi, cmap=mcolors.LinearSegmentedColormap.from_list(
    "fi_color", ["#F9EBE9", AMBER, RED]),
    vmin=9, vmax=20, s=60, alpha=0.8, edgecolors="white",
    linewidth=0.5, zorder=4)

# extremes
highlight = {"AR":"AR","MS":"MS","NM":"NM","LA":"LA","WV":"WV",
             "ND":"ND","NH":"NH","MA":"MA","MD":"MD","MN":"MN"}
for s, label in highlight.items():
    if s in state_fi and s in state_poverty:
        ax4.annotate(label,
                     xy=(state_poverty[s], state_fi[s]),
                     xytext=(state_poverty[s]+0.15, state_fi[s]+0.3),
                     fontsize=8, fontweight="bold", color=RED if state_fi[s]>16 else MUTED)

# Regression
slope, intercept, r, p, _ = stats.linregress(pov, fi)
x_line = np.linspace(min(pov), max(pov), 100)
ax4.plot(x_line, slope*x_line + intercept, "--", color=PURPLE,
         linewidth=2.0, zorder=3, label=f"Trend  (r = {r:.2f})")

ax4.set_xlabel("State poverty rate (%)", fontsize=10, color=MUTED)
ax4.set_ylabel("State food insecurity rate (%)", fontsize=10, color=MUTED)
ax4.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda v, _: f"{v:.0f}%"))
ax4.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda v, _: f"{v:.0f}%"))
ax4.legend(fontsize=9.5, framealpha=0.7, edgecolor=BORDER)
ax4.spines[["top","right"]].set_visible(False)
ax4.spines[["left","bottom"]].set_color(BORDER)
ax4.set_axisbelow(True)

cbar = fig4.colorbar(sc, ax=ax4, fraction=0.025, pad=0.01, shrink=0.8)
cbar.set_label("Food insecurity rate", fontsize=8.5, color=MUTED)
cbar.ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda v, _: f"{v:.0f}%"))
cbar.ax.tick_params(labelsize=8, colors=MUTED)
cbar.outline.set_visible(False)

fig4.text(0.08, 0.97, "STORY 6  ·  DATA 608  ·  KNOWLEDGE & VISUAL ANALYTICS",
          fontsize=8.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig4.transFigure)
fig4.text(0.08, 0.925, "Where poverty goes, hunger follows — a near-perfect relationship across states",
          fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", va="top", transform=fig4.transFigure)
fig4.text(0.08, 0.875,
          "State poverty rate vs. food insecurity rate  |  Each dot = one state  |  r = correlation strength",
          fontsize=9.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig4.transFigure)
fig4.text(0.08, 0.02,
          "Sources: USDA ERS 2022-2024 avg; US Census Bureau American Community Survey 2022-2024",
          fontsize=7.5, color=MUTED, va="bottom", transform=fig4.transFigure)

plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.04, 1, 0.865])
plt.show()
```

**The correlation is clear and strong.** States with higher poverty rates consistently have higher food insecurity rates. This is not a coincidence — it is the same causal chain playing out in 50 different policy environments. Reducing poverty reduces hunger. The policy implication is direct.

\newpage

## The Cost of Inaction: What Happens to Food-Insecure Children?

This is the question that demands a political answer. The assignment does not end when a child goes to bed hungry. It compounds — in the classroom, in the doctor's office, and in the workforce years later.

```{python}
#| label: fig-child-pipeline
#| fig-cap: "The long-term pipeline from childhood food insecurity to adult outcomes. Each bar shows the increased risk multiplier compared to food-secure peers. Sources: peer-reviewed research, 2015-2024."
#| fig-height: 5.8
#| fig-width: 10

outcomes = [
    "Fair/poor\nhealth (child)",
    "Asthma\n(child)",
    "Developmental\ndelays (child)",
    "Poor academic\nperformance",
    "Behavioral\nproblems",
    "Adult\ndepression",
    "Chronic disease\n(adult)",
    "Requires public\nassistance (adult)",
]
# risk multipliers vs food-secure peers (peer-reviewed literature)
multipliers = [2.0, 1.4, 1.6, 1.8, 1.7, 1.5, 1.4, 1.9]
phases = ["Child outcomes"]*5 + ["Adult outcomes"]*3
phase_colors = [BLUE]*5 + [PURPLE]*3

fig5, ax5 = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5.8))
fig5.patch.set_facecolor(BG)

x = range(len(outcomes))
bars = ax5.barh(list(x), multipliers, color=phase_colors,
                height=0.6, alpha=0.85, zorder=3)

# reference line at 1.0  - no difference
ax5.axvline(1.0, color=GREEN, linewidth=1.8, zorder=4,
            linestyle="--", label="Baseline: food-secure peers (1.0x)")

for i, (bar, val) in enumerate(zip(bars, multipliers)):
    ax5.text(val + 0.03, bar.get_y() + bar.get_height()/2,
             f"{val:.1f}x more likely",
             va="center", fontsize=9, fontweight="bold",
             color=BLUE if i < 5 else PURPLE)

ax5.set_yticks(list(x))
ax5.set_yticklabels(outcomes, fontsize=10)
ax5.set_xlabel("Risk multiplier vs. food-secure peers", fontsize=10, color=MUTED)
ax5.set_xlim(0.5, 2.8)
ax5.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda v, _: f"{v:.1f}x"))
ax5.set_axisbelow(True)
ax5.spines[["top","right"]].set_visible(False)
ax5.spines[["left","bottom"]].set_color(BORDER)

legend_items = [
    mpatches.Patch(color=BLUE,   label="Child outcomes"),
    mpatches.Patch(color=PURPLE, label="Adult outcomes"),
    mpatches.Patch(color=GREEN,  label="Baseline: food-secure peers"),
]
ax5.legend(handles=legend_items, loc="lower right", fontsize=9,
           framealpha=0.7, edgecolor=BORDER)

fig5.text(0.08, 0.97, "STORY 6  ·  DATA 608  ·  KNOWLEDGE & VISUAL ANALYTICS",
          fontsize=8.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig5.transFigure)
fig5.text(0.08, 0.925,
          "A hungry child today becomes a costly public health problem tomorrow",
          fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", va="top", transform=fig5.transFigure)
fig5.text(0.08, 0.875,
          "Increased risk of negative outcomes for food-insecure vs food-secure children  |  Peer-reviewed research",
          fontsize=9.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig5.transFigure)
fig5.text(0.08, 0.02, SOURCE_PEER,
          fontsize=7.5, color=MUTED, va="bottom", transform=fig5.transFigure)

plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.04, 1, 0.865])
plt.show()
```

**The research is unambiguous.** Food-insecure children are:

- **2x more likely** to be in fair or poor health
- **1.8x more likely** to perform poorly in school
- **1.9x more likely** to require public assistance as adults

A child who cannot concentrate in school because they are hungry does not disappear from the public ledger, they re-enter it as an adult requiring healthcare, support services, and reduced tax contributions.

\newpage

## What Programs Exist — and Are They Enough?

```{python}
#| label: fig-programs
#| fig-cap: "Federal nutrition assistance program spending, FY2024. SNAP alone served 42 million Americans monthly. Source: USDA FNS Annual Report FY2024."
#| fig-height: 4.8
#| fig-width: 10

programs = [
    "SNAP\n(food stamps)",
    "National School\nLunch Program",
    "WIC\n(women/infants/children)",
    "School\nBreakfast",
    "Child & Adult\nCare Food",
    "Summer\nFood Service",
    "Other\nprograms",
]
spending_b = [101.4, 17.2, 7.5, 5.8, 4.1, 0.9, 5.3]  
participants_m = [42.1, 29.6, 6.7, 15.3, 4.5, 2.9, None]

colors_prog = [RED, BLUE, AMBER, BLUE, BLUE, BLUE, MUTED]

fig6, ax6 = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 4.8))
fig6.patch.set_facecolor(BG)

x = range(len(programs))
bars = ax6.bar(x, spending_b, color=colors_prog, width=0.65,
               zorder=3, alpha=0.88)

for i, (bar, val, part) in enumerate(zip(bars, spending_b, participants_m)):
    ax6.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, val + 0.8,
             f"${val:.1f}B",
             ha="center", va="bottom", fontsize=9,
             fontweight="bold", color=colors_prog[i])
    if part:
        ax6.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, val/2,
                 f"{part:.0f}M\npeople",
                 ha="center", va="center", fontsize=8,
                 color="white", fontweight="bold")

ax6.set_xticks(list(x))
ax6.set_xticklabels(programs, fontsize=9.5, rotation=15, ha="right")
ax6.set_ylabel("Federal spending ($ billions, FY2024)", fontsize=10, color=MUTED)
ax6.set_ylim(0, 120)
ax6.set_axisbelow(True)
ax6.spines[["top","right"]].set_visible(False)
ax6.spines[["left","bottom"]].set_color(BORDER)

fig6.text(0.08, 0.97, "STORY 6  ·  DATA 608  ·  KNOWLEDGE & VISUAL ANALYTICS",
          fontsize=8.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig6.transFigure)
fig6.text(0.08, 0.925,
          "$142 billion spent — yet 47 million Americans still go hungry",
          fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", va="top", transform=fig6.transFigure)
fig6.text(0.08, 0.875,
          "Federal nutrition assistance program spending, FY2024  |  Participant counts shown inside bars",
          fontsize=9.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig6.transFigure)
fig6.text(0.08, 0.02,
          "Source: USDA Food and Nutrition Service Annual Report FY2024",
          fontsize=7.5, color=MUTED, va="bottom", transform=fig6.transFigure)

plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.04, 1, 0.865])
plt.show()
```

The federal government spends **$142 billion annually** on nutrition assistance. Yet food insecurity is rising. The programs exist — but gaps in eligibility, benefit cliffs, and funding cuts are leaving millions behind. **66% of food-insecure Americans earn above the federal poverty line** and are ineligible for most assistance.

---

## The Policy Ask: Three Actions That Would Move the Needle

This story makes three evidence-based recommendations for the legislators in this room:

**(1) Expand SNAP eligibility thresholds.** The current 130% of poverty line cutoff excludes millions of working families who are food insecure but not poor enough to qualify. Raising the threshold to 200% would cover an additional 14.5 million people.

**(2) Protect and expand child nutrition programs.** The National School Lunch and Breakfast programs are among the highest-return public investments available — every $1 spent on school meals returns an estimated $\$$2.48 in long-term economic benefit through improved educational and health outcomes.

**(3) Address the poverty-hunger link directly.** Because poverty and food insecurity are so tightly correlated, policies that reduce poverty — earned income tax credits, housing assistance, childcare support — also reduce hunger. These are not separate policy problems.

---

## References

Rabbitt, M.P., Reed-Jones, M., Hales, L.J., Suttles, S., & Burke, M.P. (2025). *Household food security in the United States in 2024* (ERR-358). USDA Economic Research Service. <https://doi.org/10.32747/2025.8583176.ers>

Rabbitt, M.P., Reed-Jones, M., Hales, L.J., & Burke, M.P. (2024). *Household food security in the United States in 2023* (ERR-337). USDA ERS. <https://doi.org/10.32747/2024.8583175.ers>

Feeding America. (2024). *Map the Meal Gap 2024: Food insecurity and food costs in the United States.* <https://www.feedingamerica.org/research/map-the-meal-gap>

Gundersen, C., & Ziliak, J.P. (2015). Food insecurity and health outcomes. *Health Affairs*, 34(11), 1830–1839. <https://doi.org/10.1377/hlthaff.2015.0645>

Fertig, A. (2020). *The long-term health consequences of childhood food insecurity.* University of Kentucky Center for Poverty Research. <https://cpr.uky.edu/research/authors/angela-fertig>

Gaitán-Rossi, P. et al. (2024). Meta-review of child and adolescent experiences and consequences of food insecurity. *Global Food Security*, 41. <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gfs.2024.100748>

U.S. Department of Agriculture, Food and Nutrition Service. (2025). *FNS Annual Report FY2024.* <https://www.fns.usda.gov/annual-summary>

U.S. Census Bureau. (2024). *American Community Survey 2022–2024 estimates.* <https://www.census.gov/programs-surveys/acs>
